Tuning Tips for Oracle Databases

Oracle database can be tuned to improve performance with a Uniface application.

Increasing the Number of Open Cursors

In Uniface, you use assignment settings to change the number of open cursors for Oracle. Ensure that the client assignment file contains the following assignment under [DRIVER_SETTINGS]:

USYS$ORA_PARAMS = oc 300, dhfr

Modifying the oc value increases the number of open cursors available for the Oracle SQL*Net driver to use during transactions with Uniface. Without this setting, SQL*Net uses the Oracle default of 45 open cursors.

Avoid using the following USYS$ORA_PARAMS settings.

  • dc—Skips the run-time consistency checks for the storage formats when the table is opened
  • dri—Disables the use of row IDs

Oracle itself contains parameters for setting a maximum limit of open cursors. This setting is defined in the Oracle Initialization file (INIT.ORA). The value is defined as a per-user setting and should be modified by the database administrator.

Increasing Virtual Memory

Insufficient virtual memory is a common cause of system crashes in Oracle DBMS. Therefore, increasing virtual memory size usually improves performance. The recommended virtual memory size is three times the physical RAM size of the computer. For Citrix or Terminal Server applications, more memory might be required.

Using Lightweight Sessions

In Oracle, applications can have multiple user sessions within a single database session. OCI enables a middle tier to set up a number of lightweight user sessions within a single database connection. Each user session uniquely identifies a connected user. These lightweight sessions reduce the network overhead of creating separate network connections from the middle tier to the database. The application can switch between these sessions as required to process transactions on behalf of users.

Disabling Optimizer Hint

Uniface's Oracle connector generates the optimizer hint FIRST_ROWS whenever appropriate. This feature relies on a stored procedure called dbms_utility.analyze_schema to analyze table statistics on a regular basis. If the statistics don't exist, Oracle tries to create them on the fly, which can slow performance considerably. To improve performance, you can either run the stored procedure or set the following assignment in the Uniface assignment file:

ORA$PARAMS = disable hint first_rows
